does: This paper introduces Alignment Vectors, a method for controlling LLM behavior at inference time by learning encoded representations of preference dimensions through model subtraction. The core contribution is computing preference representations as differences between base and aligned models, enabling users to dynamically adjust model outputs across preference spectra during inference using simple linear operations. The authors demonstrate the approach across three domains—medical, legal, and financial—with three granular preference levels per domain, showing that this method provides flexible control without requiring access to a reward model at each inference step or full model retraining when preferences change.
connects: This work builds on the intersection of model editing techniques and inference-time alignment research. It extends prior work on RLHF and training-time alignment methods by shifting the alignment operation to inference, responding to the computational inefficiency of retraining entire models for preference changes. The approach relates to recent research on steering model behavior through learned representations and interpretability work on model internals, particularly the use of model arithmetic for capturing semantic differences. The paper also connects to growing interest in personalizable and adaptable LLMs that can serve diverse user preferences without multiple specialized model checkpoints.
enables: This framework opens several research directions toward more flexible and user-centric LLM deployment. It enables development of richer preference spaces beyond the three demonstrated domains, supporting continuous preference adjustment rather than discrete model variants. The work points toward personalized LLM systems where users can fine-tune model behavior in real-time according to context-specific needs, potentially extending to multi-objective preference optimization. It also suggests future investigation into compositional preference representations, combining multiple alignment vectors, and applying similar inference-time control mechanisms to other model properties like factuality, safety, and style.
